The result: false passes (combinations that technically pass but are hard to read) and false fails (combinations that are perfectly legible but flunk the math). Dark mode designs suffer particularly — the formula was calibrated for light backgrounds and systematically penalizes light-on-dark text.

Designers have worked around this for years with informal rules like "bump it to 7:1 for small text" or "just make dark mode text slightly brighter." APCA makes those heuristics unnecessary by encoding perceptual science directly into the algorithm.

How APCA Works Differently

APCA outputs a score from roughly 0 to 106 (not a ratio) that represents perceived lightness difference between foreground and background. Critically, the required score varies by font size and weight — a large, bold heading needs less contrast to be readable than 14px body text. The algorithm also accounts for polarity: light text on dark backgrounds is evaluated differently than dark text on light backgrounds, reflecting how human vision actually processes these configurations.

The practical upshot: your design system's color palette might pass WCAG 2.x checks while failing APCA for small text — or vice versa. APCA is already available in Figma plugins like Visual Contrast and Evil Martians' Polychrom, so you can audit your existing palette without waiting for the spec to become official.

The Bronze/Silver/Gold Scoring Model

WCAG 3.0 also replaces the A/AA/AAA conformance levels with a graduated scoring system. Bronze is the new baseline, roughly equivalent to current Level AA. Silver requires higher average scores across more guidelines, representing "genuinely good accessibility." Gold is aspirational, covering cognitive accessibility guidelines that WCAG 2.x barely addressed — ADHD-friendly layouts, clear language, consistent navigation, and enhanced error recovery.

This graduated model means partial credit is possible for the first time. A site that nails keyboard accessibility and color contrast but struggles with cognitive guidelines gets scored proportionally rather than simply "failing." For product teams, this changes the conversation from "are we compliant?" to "where are we on the spectrum, and what's the highest-impact improvement?"

Cognitive Accessibility: The New Frontier

WCAG 2.x was built around sensory and motor disabilities — screen readers, keyboard navigation, color blindness. WCAG 3.0 adds substantive guidelines for cognitive disabilities including clear language requirements, consistent navigation patterns, redundant form entry prevention, and enhanced error handling. These aren't new ideas to good UX practitioners, but formalizing them in an accessibility standard means they'll become auditable requirements rather than nice-to-haves.

What to Do Before 2028

Full regulatory adoption of WCAG 3.0 isn't expected before 2028-2030. But three moves are worth making now. First, audit your color system against APCA using existing Figma plugins — know where your gaps are before they become compliance issues. Second, document your typography scale alongside contrast requirements, since APCA makes font size and weight part of the equation. Third, start cognitive accessibility testing with users who have ADHD, dyslexia, or memory impairments — this is the area where most teams have the largest blind spot and the longest ramp-up time.

中文版

十多年来，4.5:1 对比度比率是每个设计师都背得出的无障碍数字。两个颜色扔进检查器，达标就过关。但 WCAG 3.0——目前仍是工作草案，预计 2027 年进入候选推荐——正在用 APCA（Advanced Perceptual Contrast Algorithm）取代这个公式，其对设计系统色彩方案的影响足够深远，值得现在就开始准备。

当前 WCAG 2.x 的对比度公式把所有达到相同数值比率的颜色组合视为等价，完全忽略字号、字重，以及人类感知亮度差异的非线性特征。结果就是：假通过（技术上达标但难以阅读）和假失败（完全可读但数学不过关）。暗色模式尤其吃亏——公式是为浅色背景校准的，系统性地惩罚亮字暗底的文本。

APCA 输出一个约 0 到 106 的分数（不是比率），代表前景与背景之间的感知亮度差异。关键在于，所需分数随字号和字重变化——大号粗体标题比 14px 正文需要的对比度低得多。算法还区分极性：浅字暗底和暗字浅底的评估方式不同，反映了人类视觉的真实处理方式。

WCAG 3.0 同时用 Bronze/Silver/Gold 分级取代 A/AA/AAA。Bronze 大致相当于现有的 Level AA；Silver 要求更高的平均分和更广的指南覆盖；Gold 涵盖认知无障碍——ADHD 友好布局、清晰语言、一致导航——这些 WCAG 2.x 几乎没有触及的领域。分级模型意味着首次可以"部分得分"，产品团队的对话从"我们合规吗？"变成"我们在光谱上的什么位置，最高价值的改进是什么？"

WCAG 3.0 的全面法规采纳预计要到 2028-2030 年，但三件事现在就值得做：用 Figma 插件（如 Visual Contrast 或 Polychrom）对现有色彩系统做 APCA 审计；把排版体系和对比度要求一起文档化（因为 APCA 把字号字重纳入方程）；开始认知无障碍测试——这是多数团队盲区最大、爬坡时间最长的领域。

Sources:

  • https://web-accessibility-checker.com/en/blog/wcag-3-0-guide-2026-changes-prepare
  • https://abilitynet.org.uk/resources/digital-accessibility/what-expect-wcag-30-web-content-accessibility-guidelines
  • https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1090308131937420683/visual-contrast-everything-clearly-with-apca
  • https://github.com/evilmartians/figma-polychrom

item.title: 🎲 费米午餐时随口一问「大家都在哪？」，人类花了75年还没答上来
item.description:
1950年夏天，Los Alamos国家实验室。物理学家Enrico Fermi和同事Emil Konopinski、Edward Teller、Herbert York走向食堂吃午饭，路上聊到最近《纽约客》杂志上一幅关于外星人偷垃圾桶的漫画。话题从飞碟聊到了光速旅行的可能性，然后Fermi突然问了一句——后来的人对措辞有分歧，Teller记得是"Where is everybody?"，York记得是"Don't you ever wonder where everybody is?"——但桌上所有人都立刻明白他在问什么。据Teller回忆，"所有人都笑了，因为大家马上理解了他说的是外星生命。"

Fermi的推理很简单也很致命：银河系有上千亿颗恒星，很多比太阳老得多。如果哪怕极小比例的恒星有宜居行星，如果生命和智慧文明哪怕不算罕见，那么在数十亿年的时间里，应该早就有文明发展出星际旅行能力并造访地球了。可是——没有。没有信号，没有探测器，没有痕迹。他在午餐桌上做了一系列快速的概率估算，结论是："他们早该在这了。"

这个问题后来被称为费米悖论（Fermi Paradox），1975年由Michael Hart正式系统化。1961年，天文学家Frank Drake提出了一个方程式来量化这个问题：把恒星形成率、宜居行星比例、生命出现概率、智慧演化概率、文明持续时间等七个变量乘在一起。问题在于，后四个变量至今完全不可知。Drake方程优雅但无法求解——它更像一个「我们不知道什么」的清单。

75年来，科学家提出了几十种解释，几个最有影响力的：

Robin Hanson 1996年提出大过滤器（Great Filter）假说：从无生命物质到星际文明之间存在一个极难跨越的门槛。如果这个门槛在我们身后（比如真核细胞的出现本身就是极小概率事件），那我们很幸运；如果在我们前方（比如文明在获得星际能力前总会自毁），那我们很危险。2024年，有研究者提出AI可能就是大过滤器——生物文明普遍低估AI的发展速度，来不及应对就被取代。

暗黑森林（Dark Forest）假说源自刘慈欣2008年的小说《黑暗森林》（虽然类似概念更早就有人提过）：宇宙是一片黑暗森林，每个文明都是带枪的猎人。暴露自己位置的文明会被更先进的文明消灭，所以所有幸存的文明都选择沉默。这个假说在博弈论框架下有一定逻辑自洽性——但它假设「先发制人」永远比「合作」更优，而地球生物演化史并不完全支持这个前提。

动物园假说（Zoo Hypothesis）：高级文明故意不干涉地球，像我们观察自然保护区一样。稀有地球假说（Rare Earth）：复杂生命需要的条件组合极端苛刻——银河系位置、恒星类型、磁层、板块构造、大型卫星（月球稳定地轴）——地球可能真的是极端特例。

2021年，Robin Hanson提出了「贪婪外星人」（Grabby Aliens）模型：快速扩张的文明会以接近光速重塑周围空间，我们观测不到它们恰恰说明人类文明出现得很早——宇宙还没被"占满"。

最让人不安的或许不是任何一个答案，而是这个问题本身。Fermi那天午餐后没再多说什么。但他用几秒钟的直觉问出的问题，75年来仍然是人类面对宇宙时最诚实的困惑。

Sources:

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermi_paradox
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_forest_hypothesis
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Filter
  • https://orbitaltoday.com/2025/02/22/the-great-filter-theory-answer-to-the-fermi-paradox/

item.title: 🎨 Agentic Design System Governance: AI That Watches Your Components for Drift / 智能体化的设计系统治理：让 AI 替你盯住组件漂移
item.description:
Design systems have a governance problem that no amount of Confluence pages can solve. Teams build beautiful token hierarchies and component libraries, then watch them erode as designers detach components, developers hard-code overrides, and nobody updates the documentation. The Zeroheight 2026 Design Systems Report (which we covered on March 27) quantified the gap: teams trust their design system, they just don't use it consistently.

A new wave of tooling — covered extensively at the AI Design Systems Conference 2026 (Into Design Systems) and in practical guides from teams at Parallel and STL Digital — proposes a structural fix: AI agents that continuously monitor design files and code repositories for drift, flag deviations, and in some cases auto-correct them.

The Architecture: MCP as the Nervous System

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the connective tissue making this possible. An MCP-connected agent can read your Figma file's token values, compare them against the canonical design system definition, and flag any component instance where a designer has overridden a spacing token or swapped a non-system color. The same agent can watch your code repository — scanning React components, for instance — and detect when a developer has hard-coded padding: 16px instead of referencing var(--space-4).

This is more than linting. Traditional linters check static rules. An agent with MCP access understands the intent of the design system — it knows that --space-4 is the correct token for button padding because it can read the component specification, not just the CSS. When the design system updates (say, button padding changes from 16px to 12px globally), the agent can propagate that change across every downstream file, or at minimum flag every instance that needs manual review.

Metrics That Justify the Investment

The numbers from early adopters are striking. A 2025 peer-reviewed study cited by Parallel's Robin Dhanwani reports a 62% reduction in design inconsistencies, 82% reduction in design-related technical debt, and 43% fewer design-related bug reports for teams using AI-driven governance. Component validation systems can process 1,200 interface elements per minute with 94.3% accuracy — a pace no human auditor can match.

These are impressive numbers, and they deserve some skepticism. The "62% reduction" likely compares against teams with no governance tooling at all, not teams with mature manual review processes. Still, even discounting by half, the efficiency gain is real: the bottleneck in design system governance has always been the labor cost of auditing, not the difficulty of writing rules.

Three Governance Patterns Emerging

1. Passive monitoring. The agent scans design files and codebases on a schedule (or on commit), generates a report of deviations, and surfaces it in Slack or a dashboard. No automated changes — just visibility. This is the lowest-risk entry point and where most teams should start.

2. Gated enforcement. The agent runs as a CI check. Pull requests that introduce non-system tokens or detached components get flagged in code review. Design files that break system constraints trigger a notification to the design system team. The agent suggests fixes but doesn't apply them. This is the pattern STL Digital recommends for teams with established change-log practices.

3. Auto-correction with human approval. The agent detects drift, proposes a fix (e.g., "replace hard-coded #0066CC with {colors.primary}"), and opens a draft PR or Figma suggestion. A human reviews and merges. One team at the AI Design Systems Conference described this as "governance as a pull request" — the agent's changes are versioned, reviewable, and reversible.

The Risk Nobody Talks About

Auto-correction sounds appealing until you consider that not every deviation is a mistake. Sometimes a designer detaches a component because the system's component genuinely doesn't support the use case. An overly aggressive governance agent treats all deviation as drift, when some of it is legitimate design exploration that should feed back into the system as new patterns. The best implementations include an "override with rationale" mechanism — the designer can mark a deviation as intentional and explain why, which the agent then routes to the design system team as a potential enhancement request rather than a violation.

Robin Dhanwani at Parallel puts it simply: "Keep humans in the loop. Use machines as assistants, not decision-makers." The agents that work are the ones that amplify the design system team's capacity for oversight, not the ones that replace their judgment.

中文版

设计系统有一个靠 Confluence 页面解决不了的治理问题。团队搭建了漂亮的 token 层级和组件库，然后眼看着它们被侵蚀——设计师脱离组件、开发者硬编码覆盖值、没人更新文档。Zeroheight 2026 报告（我们在 3 月 27 日已报道）量化了这个缺口：团队信任设计系统，只是不一致地使用它。

一波新工具——在 AI Design Systems Conference 2026（Into Design Systems 主办）和来自 Parallel、STL Digital 的实践指南中被广泛讨论——提出了结构性解法：让 AI 智能体持续监控设计文件和代码仓库的漂移，标记偏差，某些情况下自动修正。

Model Context Protocol（MCP）是让这一切成为可能的连接组织。一个接入 MCP 的智能体可以读取 Figma 文件的 token 值，与规范的设计系统定义对比，标记任何覆盖了间距 token 或替换了非系统颜色的组件实例。同一个智能体还能监控代码仓库——比如扫描 React 组件——检测开发者在哪里硬编码了 padding: 16px 而没有引用 var(--space-4)。

这不只是 linting。传统 linter 检查静态规则，而有 MCP 访问权限的智能体理解设计系统的意图——它知道 --space-4 是按钮内边距的正确 token，因为它能读取组件规格说明，不只是 CSS。

早期采用者的数据不容忽视：设计不一致减少 62%、设计相关技术债减少 82%、设计相关 bug 报告减少 43%。当然这些数字大概率是和没有治理工具的团队比较，值得打折看待。但即使对半砍，效率提升也是实在的：设计系统治理的瓶颈一直是审计的人力成本，不是写规则的难度。

三种治理模式正在浮现：被动监控（定期扫描、生成报告、不自动改动）、门控执行（作为 CI 检查运行、在 PR 评审中标记偏差）、以及带人工审批的自动修正（智能体检测漂移并开一个 draft PR 提交修复建议）。最后一种被称作"治理即 Pull Request"——智能体的变更有版本、可审查、可回滚。

但自动修正有一个很少被讨论的风险：并非所有偏差都是错误。有时设计师脱离组件是因为系统组件确实不支持当前用例。好的实现会包含一个"带理由的覆盖"机制——设计师可以标记偏差为有意为之并解释原因，智能体将其路由给设计系统团队作为潜在的增强请求，而非违规。Parallel 的 Robin Dhanwani 说得直白："让人留在回路里。把机器当助手，不是决策者。"

Sources:

  • https://www.parallelhq.com/blog/automating-design-systems-with-ai
  • https://www.stldigital.tech/blog/ai-as-a-design-system-governor-enforcing-architectural-consistency/
  • https://www.intodesignsystems.com
